content.text: Every Python web framework sits on top of one of two server interface standards: WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface) or ASGI (Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface). Understanding both is foundational for anyone building agent backends, because the choice of interface determines whether your server can handle concurrent LLM calls efficiently or whether each request will block a thread waiting for an API response.
What it is
WSGI (PEP 3333) is a Python standard that defines how a web server communicates with a web application. The contract is simple: a WSGI app is a callable that accepts
environ
(a dict of request info) and
start_response
(a callback to set the status and headers), and returns an iterable of response body bytes. It was introduced in 2003 and underpins Flask, Django (traditional), and most legacy Python services.
ASGI (defined by the Django Channels project, later standardized) is the modern successor. An ASGI application is an
async def
callable that receives three arguments:
scope
(connection metadata),
receive
(an async callable to pull incoming events), and
send
(an async callable to emit outgoing events). ASGI supports HTTP, WebSockets, lifespan events, and HTTP/2 within a single interface.
Why it matters

Agent backends spend most of their wall-clock time waiting: waiting for LLM API responses (often 2–30 seconds), waiting for tool calls, waiting for vector database queries. Under WSGI, each of those waits pins an OS thread. A Gunicorn worker pool of 8 synchronous threads can serve at most 8 concurrent in-flight requests regardless of how much CPU sits idle.

Under ASGI with an asyncio event loop, a single thread can interleave thousands of concurrent coroutines. While one coroutine awaits the LLM response, the event loop runs other coroutines. This maps almost perfectly to how agent workloads look in production: high concurrency, low CPU usage, long I/O waits.
Benefits
WSGI benefits
Extremely mature — over 20 years of battle-hardened tooling, middleware, and hosting support.
Simple mental model: one function call per HTTP request, no event loop to reason about.
Any blocking code (SQLAlchemy queries, subprocess calls) works without wrappers.
Excellent debugger support — standard Python tracebacks, no async stack frames.
Huge middleware ecosystem: Flask extensions, Django middleware, WSGI auth layers all compose easily.
ASGI benefits
Handles thousands of concurrent slow requests on a single thread using asyncio.
Native WebSocket and Server-Sent Events (SSE) support — essential for streaming LLM tokens.
Lifespan events let you initialize and teardown resources (database pools, model caches) cleanly.
Lower memory footprint per concurrent request versus thread-per-request WSGI.
Frameworks like FastAPI, Starlette, and Litestar are built natively on ASGI.
Trade-offs
WSGI limitations
Concurrency scales only by adding OS threads or processes — expensive at high load.
No WebSocket support without bolting on a separate layer (e.g., Flask-SocketIO with gevent).
Streaming responses are possible but clunky — generators returned from view functions, not async yields.
Thread blocking is invisible — forgetting to off-load CPU work silently degrades all other requests.
ASGI limitations
Accidentally blocking the event loop (synchronous DB calls, time.sleep) stalls all concurrent tasks.
Async stack traces are harder to read; debuggers and profilers need async-aware tooling.
Many legacy libraries (some ORMs, LDAP clients, payment SDKs) are synchronous-only — must run in a thread pool via run_in_executor.
Greater cognitive overhead: you must think about what is async and what is sync at every layer.
How it works
The WSGI call cycle
When a request arrives, the WSGI server (Gunicorn, uWSGI) picks an available worker process or thread and calls
app(environ, start_response)
. The application logic runs synchronously to completion: it reads from
environ
, calls
start_response(status, headers)
, and returns an iterable of byte strings. The worker is tied up for the full duration — it cannot accept another request until
app()
returns.
The ASGI event cycle
The ASGI server (Uvicorn, Hypercorn) maintains a single asyncio event loop. When a connection arrives, it creates an
asyncio.Task
that calls
await app(scope, receive, send)
. When the coroutine hits an
await

expression (waiting for a network call, a file read, or the next WebSocket frame), it yields control back to the event loop. The loop immediately picks another task to run. This cooperative multitasking means thousands of concurrent connections coexist on a single OS thread — as long as nothing blocks.
The
scope
dict identifies the connection type (
"http"
"websocket"
, or
"lifespan"
) and carries metadata like path, method, and headers.
receive()
yields the next event from the client (request body chunk, WebSocket message).
send()
pushes events to the client (response start, body chunk, WebSocket message).
Architecture

The diagram below shows the request lifecycle side by side. WSGI (left, orange) binds one thread per request — the thread blocks while the app callable runs. ASGI (right, cyan) funnels all connections through a single event loop — concurrent requests coexist as tasks that yield on each await.

attrs.alt: ASGI vs WSGI request lifecycle side-by-side diagram

content.text: When to use it
Choose WSGI when
The codebase is already Flask or Django and there is no pressing concurrency bottleneck.
The team is unfamiliar with async Python and the risk of accidental blocking is high.
All third-party dependencies (ORM, auth libraries) are synchronous-only.
The service is a background worker or batch processor — not a long-lived concurrent server.
Choose ASGI when
Routes await LLM API calls or other slow I/O, and concurrent request volume is meaningful.
You need Server-Sent Events or WebSockets for streaming LLM token output.
The framework of choice is FastAPI, Starlette, or Litestar (all ASGI-native).
You are starting a new agent microservice and can use async libraries (asyncpg, httpx, motor) throughout.
How to implement
Running a WSGI app is a one-liner. Install Gunicorn and point it at your application object:

# WSGI — Gunicorn with sync workers
pip install gunicorn flask
gunicorn myapp:app --workers 4 --bind 0.0.0.0:8000

# ASGI — Uvicorn
pip install uvicorn[standard] fastapi
uvicorn myapp:app --workers 1 --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

# ASGI in production with multiple processes (Gunicorn + Uvicorn workers)
gunicorn myapp:app -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker --workers 4 --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
Examples
Example 1: Minimal WSGI application
A WSGI app is any callable with the signature
(environ, start_response)
. The example below implements the full contract from scratch, with no framework, to make the interface concrete.

# wsgi_app.py — bare-metal WSGI callable
import json

def application(environ, start_response):
    """
    environ: dict populated by the WSGI server.
      environ['REQUEST_METHOD'] -> 'GET', 'POST', ...
      environ['PATH_INFO']       -> '/agent/run'
      environ['wsgi.input']      -> file-like body stream
    start_response: callable(status: str, response_headers: list)
    Returns: iterable of bytes.
    """
    method = environ["REQUEST_METHOD"]
    path   = environ["PATH_INFO"]

    if method == "GET" and path == "/health":
        body = json.dumps({"status": "ok"}).encode()
        start_response(
            "200 OK",
            [("Content-Type", "application/json"),
             ("Content-Length", str(len(body)))]
        )
        return [body]

    if method == "POST" and path == "/agent/run":
        try:
            length = int(environ.get("CONTENT_LENGTH", 0) or 0)
            raw    = environ["wsgi.input"].read(length)
            data   = json.loads(raw)
            result = {"echo": data.get("prompt", ""), "status": "received"}
            body   = json.dumps(result).encode()
            start_response(
                "200 OK",
                [("Content-Type", "application/json"),
                 ("Content-Length", str(len(body)))]
            )
            return [body]
        except Exception as exc:
            body = json.dumps({"error": str(exc)}).encode()
            start_response("400 Bad Request",
                           [("Content-Type", "application/json")])
            return [body]

    body = b"Not Found"
    start_response("404 Not Found", [("Content-Type", "text/plain")])
    return [body]

# Run with: gunicorn wsgi_app:application
Example 2: Minimal ASGI application
An ASGI app is an
async def
callable with the signature
(scope, receive, send)
. This example shows how the three arguments are used and how events flow through the interface.

# asgi_app.py — bare-metal ASGI callable
import json

async def application(scope, receive, send):
    """
    scope:   dict with connection type and metadata.
      scope['type']    -> 'http' | 'websocket' | 'lifespan'
      scope['method']  -> 'GET', 'POST', ...
      scope['path']    -> '/agent/run'
    receive: async callable — pull the next client event.
    send:    async callable — push an event to the client.
    """
    assert scope["type"] == "http"  # ignore WS for this demo

    method = scope["method"]
    path   = scope["path"]

    if method == "GET" and path == "/health":
        body = json.dumps({"status": "ok"}).encode()
        await send({"type": "http.response.start",
                    "status": 200,
                    "headers": [(b"content-type", b"application/json")]})
        await send({"type": "http.response.body", "body": body})
        return

    if method == "POST" and path == "/agent/run":
        body_bytes = b""
        while True:
            event = await receive()           # yields control to event loop
            body_bytes += event.get("body", b"")
            if not event.get("more_body", False):
                break

        data   = json.loads(body_bytes)
        result = {"echo": data.get("prompt", ""), "status": "received"}
        out    = json.dumps(result).encode()
        await send({"type": "http.response.start",
                    "status": 200,
                    "headers": [(b"content-type", b"application/json")]})
        await send({"type": "http.response.body", "body": out})
        return

    await send({"type": "http.response.start", "status": 404,
                "headers": [(b"content-type", b"text/plain")]})
    await send({"type": "http.response.body", "body": b"Not Found"})

# Run with: uvicorn asgi_app:application
Real-world example
Migrating a Flask agent endpoint to FastAPI

A common migration path is moving an agent API from Flask (WSGI, blocking) to FastAPI (ASGI, async). The endpoint logic is similar but the concurrency model is fundamentally different. The Flask version blocks a Gunicorn thread for the entire duration of the LLM call; the FastAPI version awaits it, freeing the event loop to handle other requests in parallel.

# --- BEFORE: Flask (WSGI) ---
# pip install flask anthropic
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
import anthropic

app = Flask(__name__)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # sync client

@app.post("/agent/run")
def run_agent():
    data   = request.get_json(force=True)
    prompt = data.get("prompt", "")
    # Blocks the Gunicorn worker thread for the full LLM response time.
    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return jsonify({"reply": message.content[0].text})

# Serve: gunicorn flask_agent:app --workers 4


# --- AFTER: FastAPI (ASGI) ---
# pip install fastapi uvicorn anthropic
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
import anthropic

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()  # async client

class AgentRequest(BaseModel):
    prompt: str

class AgentResponse(BaseModel):
    reply: str

@app.post("/agent/run", response_model=AgentResponse)
async def run_agent(req: AgentRequest) -> AgentResponse:
    # await releases the event loop thread while LLM responds.
    # Other requests run concurrently during this await.
    message = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": req.prompt}]
    )
    return AgentResponse(reply=message.content[0].text)

# Serve: uvicorn fastapi_agent:app --workers 1
# With multiple processes:
# gunicorn fastapi_agent:app -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker --workers 4
Key differences in the migration: the sync
anthropic.Anthropic()
client becomes
anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
; the view function gains
async def
; the client call gains
await
; and Gunicorn is replaced by Uvicorn (or Gunicorn with Uvicorn workers). Pydantic models replace manual
request.get_json()
and
jsonify()
parsing.
Common mistakes
Calling a synchronous HTTP library (requests.get) inside an async route. This blocks the event loop entirely for the duration of the network call, stalling all other coroutines.
Using time.sleep() instead of await asyncio.sleep() inside async code — same problem: a blocking call holding the event loop hostage.
Running a synchronous ORM (SQLAlchemy Core without the async extension) in an async route without wrapping it in asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor().
Using multiple event loops across the same process — common when mixing uvicorn and celery worker threads. Keep each process to one event loop.

Deploying an ASGI app with plain Gunicorn sync workers. Sync Gunicorn workers do not run an event loop, so async def handlers never await anything — the code runs but blocks like WSGI. Use uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker.
Forgetting that WSGI cannot natively handle WebSockets. Adding Flask-SocketIO patches over this but uses gevent monkey-patching, which has subtle compatibility issues.
Best practices
For new agent services: default to ASGI (FastAPI + Uvicorn). The async model fits LLM I/O patterns exactly.
Keep the event loop clean: use httpx.AsyncClient for all outbound HTTP, asyncpg or SQLAlchemy async for databases, and aiofiles for filesystem I/O.
Off-load truly CPU-bound work (image processing, heavy computation) to a thread pool via asyncio.to_thread() so it does not block the event loop.
Use ASGI lifespan events to initialize shared resources (DB connection pools, LLM clients) once at startup rather than per-request.
In production, run 1 Uvicorn worker per CPU core via Gunicorn's UvicornWorker class — this combines the concurrency of asyncio with the parallelism of multiple processes.
Pin synchronous legacy code behind a separate WSGI service if migrating incrementally, and proxy to it over HTTP rather than mixing sync and async in one process.
Performance & scalability

For CPU-bound workloads there is no meaningful difference between WSGI and ASGI. Both will saturate CPU equally; the number of worker processes is what scales throughput. For I/O-bound workloads — which describes nearly every agent backend — ASGI wins decisively.

A typical LLM API call takes 2–10 seconds. With 4 synchronous WSGI workers you can handle at most 4 concurrent requests. With 1 ASGI event loop, you can handle hundreds or thousands of concurrent awaiting coroutines — each suspended at its
await
while the event loop services others. Memory consumption also drops: an asyncio coroutine uses kilobytes of stack versus the megabytes an OS thread requires.
Streaming responses via Server-Sent Events are another key advantage. An ASGI app can
await send()
each token chunk as it arrives from the LLM, achieving low time-to-first-byte. A synchronous WSGI generator-based stream buffers or requires chunked-encoding workarounds.
Tooling & ecosystem
WSGI servers and frameworks
Gunicorn — most widely used WSGI server; sync and eventlet/gevent worker types.
uWSGI — high-performance, feature-rich; common in older Nginx + uWSGI stacks.
Waitress — pure-Python WSGI server; good for Windows development and simple deployments.
Flask — the dominant WSGI microframework; rich extension ecosystem.
Django — full-stack WSGI framework; also has an ASGI adapter for async views.
ASGI servers and frameworks
Uvicorn — the standard ASGI server; built on uvloop and httptools for low latency.
Hypercorn — supports HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 natively; good when those protocols matter.
Daphne — Django Channels' reference ASGI server.
FastAPI — the most popular ASGI framework; built on Starlette with Pydantic validation and auto-generated OpenAPI docs.
Starlette — lightweight ASGI toolkit; forms the foundation of FastAPI; suitable for custom routing and middleware.
Litestar (formerly Starlite) — opinionated, fast ASGI framework with built-in dependency injection and OpenAPI support.
Django (async views) — Django 3.1+ supports ASGI and async view functions, although the ORM is still primarily sync.
Comparison with alternatives
The table below summarizes the key differences between WSGI and ASGI across servers, frameworks, concurrency model, and use-case fit.
Dimension
WSGI
ASGI
PEP / spec
PEP 3333 (2003, updated 2010)
ASGI spec (2018, Django Channels)
App callable
def app(environ, start_response)
async def app(scope, receive, send)
Concurrency model
Thread / process per request
asyncio event loop; cooperative multitasking
Production servers
Gunicorn, uWSGI, Waitress
Uvicorn, Hypercorn, Daphne
Primary frameworks
Flask, Django (traditional)
FastAPI, Starlette, Litestar, Django ASGI
WebSocket support
No (requires gevent/eventlet workarounds)
Yes, native
SSE / streaming
Possible via generator; clunky
First-class via async generators and send()
Best fit
Legacy apps, low-concurrency APIs, batch tools
Agent APIs, streaming endpoints, WebSocket services
Security & reliability

Neither WSGI nor ASGI introduces unique security vulnerabilities — both are transport-layer abstractions, and injection, authentication, and access-control issues live in application code, not the interface layer. However, there are operational differences to be aware of:

Slow-request DoS is easier to trigger under WSGI: a single request that sleeps for 60 seconds pins a thread. Under ASGI, a slow request only blocks its own coroutine; other requests continue normally.
Under ASGI, an unhandled exception in one coroutine does not crash other coroutines. ASGI servers isolate each connection's task.
TLS termination is handled by a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy) in front of both WSGI and ASGI — neither standard includes TLS negotiation.
Set request size limits at the reverse-proxy or application level to prevent memory exhaustion from large body uploads.
ASGI lifespan events allow clean startup and shutdown hooks, making it easier to drain in-flight requests before a rolling deploy — improving reliability.
Mental model

Think of WSGI as a telephone switchboard with a fixed number of operators. Each call gets an operator. While that operator is handling the call, they cannot take another — even if they spend most of the call on hold waiting for the other party.

Think of ASGI as a single operator managing a large switchboard with hold buttons. When a call goes on hold (awaiting the LLM API), the operator immediately picks up the next call. As each held call becomes active again, the operator returns to it. One operator handles many calls, but only because most of the time each call is just waiting.

This model breaks down only when a call requires the operator's full undivided attention — i.e., CPU-intensive work. For that, you need more operators (processes), which is why ASGI servers are often deployed with multiple worker processes even though each worker runs a single-threaded event loop.
Key takeaways
WSGI is a synchronous callable contract: one thread per request, blocking for the full duration. It is mature and simple but does not scale well for I/O-heavy agent workloads.
ASGI is an async callable contract: scope/receive/send, running on an asyncio event loop. It lets one thread handle thousands of concurrent I/O-bound requests.
Agent backends making LLM API calls are I/O-bound by nature — ASGI is the correct default for new services.
Never call synchronous blocking code inside an async route without wrapping it in a thread pool; a single blocking call stalls the entire event loop.
In production, pair Uvicorn workers with Gunicorn process management to combine async concurrency with multi-core parallelism.
WebSockets and Server-Sent Events — required for streaming LLM token output — are native to ASGI; they require workarounds under WSGI.
Migrating from Flask to FastAPI is straightforward: add async def, swap the Anthropic sync client for AsyncAnthropic, and replace Gunicorn with Uvicorn workers.
